original_abstract: Many older commentators understood the Epistle of James to address itself to Jews of the diaspora, whether Christian or not. Although few modern scholars have seriously reckoned with this possibility, much is to be said for the thesis. It makes sense for example of important features of the epistle that otherwise would remain unclear, such as its dearth of explicit Christology, its seeming lack of distinctive Christian sentiments, and its thoroughly Jewish orientation. The author was a Jewish Christian still hoping for a Christian place within the Jewish synagogue; he wished for irenic relations with those who did not confess Jesus to be the Messiah. He was thus intentionally quiescent about much for apologetical purposes, a strategy with clear parallels in other ancient Christian literature.Die Joodse agtergrond van die Jakobusbrief. Die Jakobusbrief is deur baie van die ouer kommentaarskrywers gesien as ’n selfverklarende geskrif aan die Jode van die diaspora – hetsy Christene of nie-Christene. Hoewel van die moderne navorsers heelhartig daarmee saamstem, bestaan daar heelwat twyfel oor hierdie siening. Dit maak egter sin ten opsigte van sekere belangrike beskrywings van die brief wat andersins onverklaarbaar sou wees soos die gebrek aan uitdruklike Christologie, die skynbare gebrek aan kenmerkende Christelike sentimente en die grondige Joodse oriëntering daarvan. Die skrywer was ’n Joodse Christen wat steeds gehoop het vir erkenning binne die Joodse sinagoge en versoenende verhoudings met diegene wat nie vir Jesus as die Messias erken het nie, verlang het. Dus was hy met voorbedagde rade baie stil oor baie dinge om apologetiese redes – ’n strategie wat duidelike parallelle met ander antieke Christelike literatuur toon.
humanized_abstract: Many older commentators thought the Epistle of James was written for Jews of the diaspora, whether those Jews were Christian or not. Few modern scholars have taken the idea seriously, but the idea has a lot of support. The Epistle of James shows clear signs that fit the idea. For example the Epistle of James does not give clear talk about Christ. The Epistle of James also does not show strong Christian feelings. The Epistle of James is clearly rooted in Jewish thought. In my view the evidence makes the idea very plausible. The author was a Jewish Christian who still hoped to have a Christian place inside the Jewish synagogue. He wanted peaceful relations with people who did not say that Jesus was the Messiah. He kept quiet on many issues on purpose to defend his faith. This approach also appears in other Christian writings. I find this quiet approach striking. Die Joodse agtergrond van die Jakobusbrief. Die Jakobusbrief is deur baie van die ouer kommentaarskrywers gesien as ’n selfverklarende geskrif aan die Jode van die diaspora – hetsy Christene of nie-Christene. Alhoewel moderne navorsers saamstem, is daar baie twyfel oor die siening. Vir my lyk die twyfel reg wanneer die brief bestudeer word. Die twyfel maak sin as die belangrike beskrywings van die brief oorweeg word. Sonder die beskrywings sou die brief onverklaarbaar wees. Die brief het geen duidelike Christologie nie. Die brief het geen Christelike sentimente nie. Die brief is Joods. Die skrywer was 'n Joodse Christen. Die skrywer het steeds gehoop vir erkenning in die Joodse sinagoge. Die skrywer het versoening gesoek met diegene wat Jesus nie as die Messias erken nie. Dus was hy met voorbedagde rade baie stil oor baie dinge om apologetiese redes – ’n strategie wat duidelike parallelle met ander antieke Christelike literatuur toon.
